items.html:
What anxiety is

Anxiety is a feeling of unease, worry or fear that everyone experiences from time to time. It is the body’s natural response to a situation it senses as threatening — a hospital appointment, a money worry, or waiting for news. In small amounts anxiety can be useful, keeping you alert and helping you prepare, and it usually settles once the situation has passed.

  • A normal feeling: short-lived worry before a stressful event is part of everyday life.
  • A physical response: the body readies itself for action, sometimes called the “fight or flight” response.
  • Usually passes: ordinary anxiety eases once the cause is over.

When worry becomes a disorder

For some people anxiety does not settle. It becomes an anxiety disorder when the feelings are strong, last for weeks or months, feel out of proportion to the situation, and begin to interfere with everyday life, relationships or sleep. Anxiety disorders are among the most common mental health conditions, and importantly they are treatable — with the right support, most people improve.

This course raises your awareness so you can recognise anxiety, offer kind support and signpost people towards help. It is knowledge-level learning only. It does not replace supervised, hands-on training, and it is never a substitute for professional mental health assessment, diagnosis or treatment. You support and signpost — you do not diagnose or treat.

Anxiety is common

Anxiety disorders affect a great many people across the UK and can begin at any age. The people you support are no exception. Those living with long-term illness, in pain, or coping with loss or trauma may be especially vulnerable, yet anxiety can affect anyone. Knowing how common it is helps you respond with understanding rather than surprise.

  • Anyone can be affected: anxiety does not depend on age, background or circumstances.
  • Often hidden: people may feel ashamed and hide how much they are struggling.
  • Frequently overlooked: signs can be mistaken for physical illness or simply “being a worrier”.

Why awareness matters

Because anxiety is so common and so easily missed, your awareness makes a real difference. You often spend more time with a person than any other worker, so you are well placed to notice when something changes. Someone who becomes withdrawn, restless or reluctant to leave their room may be struggling with anxiety they cannot easily put into words.

Left unrecognised, anxiety can shrink a person’s world, keeping them from activities, friendships and appointments they value. When you notice it early and encourage the right support, you help protect their independence, dignity and quality of life.

items.question: Which statement best reflects an accurate, awareness-level understanding of anxiety among the people you support?

answers.text: Anxiety is common, can affect anyone, and is often hidden or mistaken for something else.
answers.feedback: Correct. Anxiety is widespread and easily overlooked, which is exactly why your awareness matters.

answers.text: Anxiety only affects people who already have a formal mental health diagnosis.
answers.feedback: Not so. Anxiety can affect anyone, whether or not they have ever seen a professional about it.

answers.text: Older people cannot develop anxiety because it only affects younger adults.
answers.feedback: Anxiety can begin at any age. Older people and those with long-term illness may be especially vulnerable.

answers.text: If someone hides their anxiety well, there is no need to report a concern.
answers.feedback: People often hide distress. If you notice signs, it is still worth recording and reporting your concern.

items.html:
Your supportive role

Supporting someone with anxiety does not mean having clinical answers. Your role is to be a calm, reliable and compassionate presence — to recognise when someone is struggling, respond with warmth, and help them reach the right professional support. You are not there to diagnose the type of anxiety or to provide therapy.

  • Recognise: notice signs and changes in how a person seems from day to day.
  • Respond: stay calm, listen, and offer reassurance without judgement.
  • Signpost and report: encourage professional help and pass concerns on through the right channels.

Staying within your limits

Knowing the boundaries of your role protects both you and the person you support. Anxiety disorders are health conditions that qualified professionals assess and treat. If you try to counsel someone or explain away their fears, you may unintentionally cause harm or miss something serious.

  • Do not diagnose: avoid telling someone which condition they have.
  • Do not treat: leave therapy and medication decisions to professionals.
  • Do share concerns: anyone talking about self-harm or feeling unable to cope should always be escalated promptly.

items.question: A care worker’s role in anxiety is to recognise, support and signpost, rather than to diagnose or provide treatment.
items.correctMsg: Correct. You recognise, support and signpost. Diagnosis and treatment are for qualified professionals.
items.wrongMsg: Actually this is true. Your role is to recognise, support and signpost — not to diagnose or treat.

items.html:
Generalised anxiety disorder

Generalised anxiety disorder, or GAD, is ongoing anxiety that is not tied to one particular thing. A person feels worried and on edge about many different areas of life — health, money, family, small daily tasks — more days than not, over a long period. The worry feels hard to control and can be exhausting.

  • Widespread worry: anxiety spreads across many situations rather than just one.
  • Persistent: the feelings continue over weeks and months, not just a bad day.
  • Wearing: constant worry drains energy and disturbs sleep and concentration.

How it might show

Someone living with GAD may seek frequent reassurance, struggle to relax, or seem tense and irritable. They might imagine the worst outcome in ordinary situations, or avoid things in case something goes wrong. Physical signs such as headaches, tiredness and a churning stomach are common, because the body stays on alert.

You are not expected to label this as GAD. Simply notice the pattern — long-standing, wide-ranging worry that affects daily life — and treat it as a reason to offer support and encourage a conversation with the GP. Because the worry ranges so widely, it can be easy to take each concern at face value and try to solve them one by one. Recognising the underlying pattern helps you respond to the anxiety itself, with patience and steady reassurance, rather than chasing each individual worry.

items.question: Which of the following best describes generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?

answers.text: Ongoing, hard-to-control worry that spreads across many areas of life over a long period.
answers.feedback: Correct. GAD is persistent, wide-ranging worry that is difficult to control and affects daily life.

answers.text: An intense fear of one specific object or situation, such as spiders or heights.
answers.feedback: That describes a phobia. GAD is not focused on one thing but spreads across many worries.

answers.text: A single panic attack that happens once and never returns.
answers.feedback: GAD is long-standing worry rather than a one-off event. Panic attacks are a separate feature of panic disorder.

answers.text: Worry that lasts only a few minutes before a stressful event.
answers.feedback: Brief, situation-linked worry is normal anxiety. GAD is persistent and continues over weeks and months.

items.html:
Panic disorder and panic attacks

A panic attack is a sudden rush of intense fear and uncomfortable physical sensations that builds quickly and usually peaks within minutes. It can feel frightening and overwhelming — some people fear they are having a heart attack or losing control — but a panic attack is not dangerous and the feelings do pass. Panic disorder is when someone has repeated panic attacks and worries about having more.

  • Sudden and intense: fear rises rapidly, often without an obvious trigger.
  • Physical symptoms: a racing heart, shortness of breath, dizziness, sweating or shaking.
  • Frightening but not harmful: the sensations feel alarming yet cause no physical harm.

The cycle of panic

Panic attacks can lead to a fear of the fear itself. After a frightening episode, a person may begin to dread the next one and avoid places where an attack happened before. Over time this avoidance can narrow their life and, for some, lead to a fear of leaving home. Understanding this cycle helps you offer calm, patient reassurance rather than adding to the alarm.

Knowing that the physical sensations, though intense, are not a sign of physical danger allows you to stay steady and help the person feel safe. Your calm confidence is reassuring in itself, showing the person that what is happening is understood and will pass. In between attacks, a person may live with a background sense of dread, so a warm, predictable relationship with you can offer real comfort.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
items.textField: A sudden rush of intense fear that peaks within minutes is called a *panic* attack. During one, a person may feel their *heart* racing and become short of *breath*, and may fear something terrible is happening. These sensations feel alarming but are not *dangerous* and will pass.

items.html:
Phobias

A phobia is an intense, overwhelming fear of a particular object, situation or activity that is out of proportion to any real danger. Common examples include heights, enclosed spaces, needles or animals. A person will often go to great lengths to avoid what they fear, which can interfere with everyday life — for example, avoiding a medical appointment because of a fear of needles.

  • Focused fear: the anxiety centres on one specific thing.
  • Out of proportion: the fear is far greater than the actual risk.
  • Avoidance: the person arranges life around avoiding the trigger.

Social anxiety

Social anxiety disorder is a lasting and overwhelming fear of social situations, and it is much more than shyness. A person may dread everyday interactions such as eating in front of others, making phone calls or joining group activities, fearing they will be judged, embarrassed or humiliated. They may worry for days beforehand and replay the event afterwards.

For the people you support, social anxiety can lead to isolation and missed chances for connection. Gentle encouragement, respecting the person’s pace, and never pushing or embarrassing them are all ways you can help them feel safe.

items.question: A person you support refuses to join the day centre, saying they are terrified of being judged and humiliated in front of the group. This is most consistent with:

answers.text: Social anxiety — an overwhelming fear of social situations, rather than simple shyness.
answers.feedback: Correct. A lasting, overwhelming fear of being judged in social situations points to social anxiety.

answers.text: A specific phobia of an object such as needles or heights.
answers.feedback: A phobia centres on a specific object or situation. Here the fear is of being judged by others.

answers.text: Ordinary shyness that needs no understanding or support.
answers.feedback: Social anxiety is far more than shyness and can cause real distress and isolation. It deserves support.

answers.text: Deliberate refusal that is best ignored.
answers.feedback: This is genuine fear, not defiance. The person needs patience, reassurance and support at their own pace.

items.html:
OCD at awareness level

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) involves unwanted, distressing thoughts, known as obsessions, and repetitive actions, known as compulsions, that a person feels driven to carry out to ease the anxiety those thoughts cause — such as repeated checking, washing or counting. The relief is only brief, so the cycle repeats.

  • Obsessions: intrusive thoughts, images or urges that cause distress.
  • Compulsions: repeated actions or rituals carried out to reduce that distress.
  • A distressing cycle: the person often knows the ritual is excessive but feels unable to stop.

Post-traumatic stress

Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) can develop after a frightening or distressing event, such as an accident, an assault, serious illness or a wartime experience. A person may relive the event through flashbacks or nightmares, feel constantly on edge, and avoid reminders of what happened. Some of the people you support may carry trauma from many years ago.

You do not need to explore or treat these conditions. Approach the person with patience, avoid pushing them to talk about distressing memories, keep to reassuring routines, and report any signs of distress so professional support can be arranged.

items.question: It is a care worker’s job to encourage someone with PTSD to talk in detail about their traumatic memories so they can move on.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Pressing someone to relive trauma can cause harm. Offer patient reassurance and report concerns so trained professionals can help.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Encouraging someone to relive trauma can be harmful. Your role is calm support and signposting, not therapy.

items.html:
Person-centred support

Every person experiences anxiety differently, so support must fit the individual. What soothes one person may unsettle another. Person-centred support means learning what anxiety feels like for this person, what helps and what makes it worse, and shaping your care around their wishes rather than a fixed routine.

  • Know the person: learn their triggers, their warning signs and what reassures them.
  • Follow their lead: ask what would help rather than assuming.
  • Use the care plan: record and share what works so support stays consistent.

Staying calm and listening

When someone is anxious, your own manner matters enormously. Anxiety can be eased by a calm, unhurried presence, and made worse by rushing, raised voices or visible frustration. Keeping yourself steady helps the person feel safe.

  • Stay calm: speak slowly and gently, and keep your body language open and relaxed.
  • Listen actively: give the person time, show that you are listening, and reflect back what you hear.
  • Do not dismiss: never tell someone to simply “stop worrying” — acknowledge how real the feeling is.

Helping through a panic attack

Seeing someone have a panic attack can be alarming, but your calm response can make a real difference. The person is frightened and their body feels out of control. Your aim is to help them feel safe and to let the attack pass, which it will. Stay with them unless they ask you to leave, and keep your voice quiet and reassuring.

  • Stay and reassure: tell them calmly that they are safe and that this will pass.
  • Encourage slow breathing: breathe with them, gently slowing each breath.
  • Give space and privacy: reduce noise, onlookers and pressure.

Grounding techniques

Grounding helps a person reconnect with the present moment when anxiety feels overwhelming. Simple techniques can steady someone during or after a panic attack. Offer them gently, never force them, and follow whatever the person finds helpful.

  • Focus on the senses: invite them to name things they can see, hear and touch around them.
  • Steady the breath: encourage slow breaths in and longer breaths out.
  • Use calm words: short, reassuring phrases repeated gently can help more than long explanations.

Afterwards, give the person time to recover, and record what happened so their support can be reviewed.

items.question: A person you support is having a panic attack — their heart is racing and they feel they cannot breathe. What is the most helpful response?

answers.text: Stay calm, reassure them they are safe and that it will pass, and gently encourage slow breathing.
answers.feedback: Correct. A calm, reassuring presence and slow breathing help the person feel safe while the attack passes.

answers.text: Tell them firmly to pull themselves together and get on with the day.
answers.feedback: This dismisses genuine fear and can make things worse. Offer calm reassurance instead.

answers.text: Leave them completely alone at once and say nothing.
answers.feedback: Unless they ask you to step back, stay with them and reassure them — they need to feel safe, not abandoned.

answers.text: Insist they describe in detail everything that is frightening them.
answers.feedback: During an attack, pressing for detail adds pressure. Keep words short, calm and reassuring.

items.html:
Reducing triggers and respecting choices

Anxiety is often set off by particular triggers — certain places, times of day, noise, crowds or unexpected changes to routine. Learning a person’s triggers helps you reduce or prepare for them, easing anxiety before it builds. At the same time, you must respect the person’s own choices about what feels safe, even where their decisions differ from what you might choose.

  • Know the triggers: notice and record what tends to set off anxiety.
  • Prepare and adapt: give warning of changes and keep to reassuring routines.
  • Respect choices: support the person’s decisions and never force them into feared situations.

The link with physical health

Mind and body are closely connected. Anxiety can disturb sleep, appetite and energy, and it often occurs alongside physical illness or other conditions such as depression, dementia or long-term pain. A person who is unwell, in pain or newly diagnosed may become more anxious, and anxiety can in turn make physical symptoms feel worse.

Because of this overlap, always take physical health seriously too. Sudden anxiety or confusion can sometimes signal a physical problem such as an infection, so report changes so the person can be properly checked.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
items.textField: Learning what upsets a person helps you reduce their *triggers* before anxiety builds. Anxiety is closely linked with physical *health*, disturbing sleep and appetite, and it often overlaps with other long-term *conditions*. Whatever you notice, always respect the person’s own *choices* about what feels safe.

items.html:
Self-help and everyday wellbeing

Alongside professional treatment, there is a great deal that supports good mental wellbeing day to day. Small, regular habits can help a person feel calmer and more in control. As a care worker, you can gently encourage and enable these, always at the person’s pace and according to their wishes.

  • Keep active: gentle movement and fresh air can ease tension and lift mood.
  • Stay connected: contact with family, friends and others reduces isolation.
  • Rest and routine: good sleep, regular meals and predictable days all steady anxiety.

The Five Ways to Wellbeing

The Five Ways to Wellbeing are a well-known set of everyday actions that support mental health. You can weave them naturally into the support you already give.

  • Connect: encourage relationships and time with others.
  • Be active: support movement suited to the person’s ability.
  • Take notice: help the person pay attention to the present and to simple pleasures.

The remaining two are to keep learning — trying new or familiar activities — and to give, through small acts of kindness or helping others. Together these build a sense of purpose and connection.

items.question: The Five Ways to Wellbeing include connecting with others, being active, taking notice, giving, and which of the following?

answers.text: Keep learning — trying new or familiar activities that give a sense of achievement.
answers.feedback: Correct. The fifth of the Five Ways is to keep learning, which builds confidence and a sense of purpose.

answers.text: Avoiding all contact with other people to reduce stress.
answers.feedback: The opposite is true — connecting with others is one of the Five Ways. Isolation tends to worsen anxiety.

answers.text: Taking prescription medication without medical advice.
answers.feedback: Medication is only ever a matter for a professional, and never without advice. It is not one of the Five Ways.

answers.text: Staying in bed as much as possible each day.
answers.feedback: Being active is one of the Five Ways. Gentle activity supports wellbeing far more than staying in bed.

items.html:
Talking therapies and treatment

Anxiety disorders are treatable, and most people improve with the right help. Treatment is decided by professionals, but being aware of the options helps you reassure people that help exists and encourage them to seek it. No one has to simply live with severe anxiety.

  • Talking therapies: approaches such as cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) help people understand and manage anxious thoughts.
  • Medication: a GP may prescribe medication for some people, alone or alongside therapy.
  • Self-referral: in England, people can often refer themselves directly to NHS talking therapies.

Encouraging professional help

Because anxiety responds so well to treatment, encouraging someone towards professional help is one of the most valuable things you can do. Many people delay because of stigma, fear or simply not knowing where to turn. A calm, supportive conversation can be the nudge that helps.

  • Start with the GP: the family doctor is usually the first step and can arrange further support.
  • Offer practical help: you might help the person note their symptoms or prepare for an appointment, within your role.
  • Reassure, do not pressure: encourage gently and respect that the choice is theirs.

items.question: Which statement about treatment for anxiety is accurate at awareness level?

answers.text: Anxiety disorders are treatable, and encouraging someone to speak to their GP is a helpful step.
answers.feedback: Correct. Anxiety responds well to treatment, and gently encouraging a GP conversation is genuinely valuable.

answers.text: Anxiety cannot be treated, so there is little point encouraging professional help.
answers.feedback: Anxiety is very treatable. Encouraging professional help is one of the most useful things you can do.

answers.text: Care workers should decide which therapy or medication a person needs.
answers.feedback: Those decisions belong to qualified professionals. Your role is to reassure and signpost, not to prescribe.

answers.text: People should be pressured into treatment whether they want it or not.
answers.feedback: The choice is always the person’s own. Encourage gently and respect their decision.

items.html:
Recording and escalating concerns

Careful recording and timely reporting turn what you notice into support that reaches the person. Write down what you observe factually — what you saw or heard, when, and what helped — rather than your own interpretation. Share concerns through the right channels so the care plan stays current and professionals can act.

  • Record the facts: note changes in mood, behaviour, sleep or appetite clearly and without judgement.
  • Report promptly: pass concerns to your senior, the nurse or the care plan.
  • Escalate urgently when needed: any mention of self-harm or feeling unable to go on must be raised straight away.

Looking after your own wellbeing

Supporting people who are anxious or distressed can affect you too. Looking after your own mental wellbeing is not selfish — it keeps you able to care well for others. Use the same ideas you offer to others: stay connected, take breaks, and talk to someone you trust.

  • Notice your own signs: tiredness, tension or low mood are worth paying attention to.
  • Seek support: talk to your manager, a colleague or your own GP if you are struggling.
  • Use supervision: raise how you feel in supervision and team meetings.

Just as anxiety is common among the people you support, it is common among those who care for them. Taking your own wellbeing seriously, and encouraging colleagues to do the same, helps build a kinder and more sustainable team around every person you care for.

This course has raised your awareness of anxiety and how to support people with warmth and respect. It is knowledge-level learning only. It does not replace supervised, hands-on training, and it is not a substitute for professional mental health assessment, diagnosis or treatment. Recognise, support and signpost — and encourage everyone to access their GP and mental health services, because anxiety is treatable and help works.
